question: How do I join the Office Hours or live workshop if I don’t have the Zoom link?
answer_agent:
If you don’t have the Zoom link, that’s expected: the **Zoom link is only shared with instructors/presenters/TAs**.

As a student, you can join via:

- **YouTube Live** for the video stream
- **Slido** for questions — the link is pinned in the chat when the session is live
- The **announcements channel on Telegram and Slack** for the live video URL before it starts
- The **DataTalksClub YouTube channel** to watch live

Also, **don’t post questions in the chat**, since they may be missed if the room is active.

If you want, I can also tell you whether there are live sessions for every module.

question: How do I find my row on the leaderboard if I can’t see my name right away?
answer_agent:
Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — it will take you straight to your entry.

If you want to know what name is being shown publicly, open **“Edit Course Profile”**:
- **First field:** your public/display name on the leaderboard
- **Second field:** your official name for the certificate

By default, your leaderboard name is a random nickname like **“Lucid Elbakyan.”**
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!
tool_calls: [{'name': 'search', 'arguments': '{"query":"find my row on the leaderboard if I can’t see my name right away"}'}]

question: Why does the homework content keep changing in this course?
answer_agent:
The homework content can change because the homework may still be in **draft**.

According to the course FAQ:

- If the homework title contains **`[DRAFT]`**, it is **not ready yet**.
- The homework is only ready when:
  - the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
  - the title **does not** contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change, so working on it early is at your own risk because the final version may be different.

If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_agent:
Because WSL2 doesn’t use all of your PC’s RAM by default. It has its own memory limit, so the model only sees the RAM allocated to WSL2, not your full system memory.

To give WSL2 more RAM:

1. Create this file on Windows:
   `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

2. Add something like:
   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL:
   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Check inside WSL with:
   ```bash
   free
   ```

If you want, I can also help you choose a good memory value for your machine.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).
tool_calls: [{'name': 'search', 'arguments': '{"query":"WSL2 model needs more memory even though my PC has enough RAM"}'}]

question: How can I increase the RAM available to WSL2 on Windows for running a model?
answer_agent:
WSL2 does **not** automatically use all of your Windows RAM. You need to increase the memory limit for WSL2.

Do this:

1. Create a file named `.wslconfig` in your Windows user folder:
   ```ini
   C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig
   ```

2. Put this in the file:
   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL:
   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Open WSL again and check with:
   ```bash
   free -h
   ```

You can change `8GB` to whatever amount you want, as long as your Windows machine has enough free RAM.

If you want, I can also show you how to set CPU cores or swap size for WSL2.

question: After changing WSL2 memory settings, how can I check if the new RAM limit is actually applied?
answer_agent:
After changing `.wslconfig`, restart WSL with:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

Then open WSL again and check the available memory inside Linux with:

```bash
free -h
```

This will show whether the new RAM limit is being applied.
